paragraphs.text: 24 October 2025

evidence_quotes.quote: dark patterns

paragraphs.text: My name is Dr. Martin Brenncke, and I am a Senior Lecturer in Law at Aston University, Birmingham, United Kingdom. I am a legal expert in regulating dark patterns. My expertise is evidenced by academic publications and conference presentations.

section_path: Call for Evidence: Digital Fairness Act
Submission of written evidence by Martin Brenncke

paragraphs.text: I am responding to this consultation as an individual. I am happy for my response to be published.

section_path: Call for Evidence: Digital Fairness Act
Submission of written evidence by Martin Brenncke

evidence_quotes.quote: dark patterns
difficult cancellation of subscriptions
auto-renewals
free trials converted into paid subscriptions
drip pricing

paragraphs.text: I agree with the European Commission that reform is needed to strengthen consumer protection in the digital environment. In my response to this Call for Evidence, I address the issue of dark patterns. My recommendations are as follows: (1) The regulation of dark patterns under the Digital Fairness Act (DFA) should extend to all traders and protect consumers who are susceptible to the exploitation of their heuristics and biases by business practices. This includes all groups of consumers. (2) The DFA should contain (i) principles-based general prohibitions designed to address dark patterns and (ii) a clear blacklist of prohibited dark patterns. (3) The regulation of dark patterns in the DFA should recognise and capture loss of autonomy as a harm and be aligned with the normative principles used in art. 25(1) DSA. This achieves consistency with existing provisions in EU consumer and digital law that expressly regulate dark patterns. (4) Existing EU consumer and digital laws that expressly regulate dark patterns recognise six categories of autonomy violations in B2C relationships. Prohibitions of dark patterns in the DFA should align with these categories of autonomy violations to ensure full complementarity with existing EU consumer and digital laws. (5) The Commission should consider blacklisting certain dark patterns in the areas of drip pricing, difficult cancellation of subscriptions, auto-renewals and free trials converted into paid subscriptions.

section_path: Call for Evidence: Digital Fairness Act

paragraphs.text: The Fitness Check of EU consumer law on digital fairness found that despite the benefits of a principles-based approach, the broadly worded provisions in the existing consumer law

section_path: Call for Evidence: Digital Fairness Act

evidence_quotes.quote: dark patterns

paragraphs.text: Directives are not specific enough to allow them to be applied effectively to dark patterns in the digital environment. Only a limited number of dark patterns are directly prohibited in the UCPD blacklist. Whether a dark pattern amounts to a misleading action, misleading omission, aggressive commercial practice or falls under the general prohibition of art. 5(2) UCPD requires a case-by-case assessment. The outcome also depends on the notion of the average consumer, which is evolving in CJEU case law. This amalgamation of factors has led to a lack of legal certainty about the fairness or unfairness of specific types of dark patterns under the UCPD. The existing consumer law has certainly not stopped the proliferation of dark patterns.

section_path: Call for Evidence: Digital Fairness Act

evidence_quotes.quote: dark patterns
dark patterns are prevalent across the digital ecosystem.

paragraphs.text: The Fitness Check further found that the new digital rulebook (DSA, DMA, Data Act, AI Act) faces similar issues of gaps in protection and legal uncertainty for tackling dark patterns. The current digital rulebook focuses on specific types of traders (eg online platforms, providers of financial services), business models and technologies (eg AI systems). That leaves gaps in protection as dark patterns are prevalent across the digital ecosystem. Furthermore, applying the new digital rulebook in conjunction with horizontal EU consumer laws has increased regulatory complexity and uncertainty. The current formulation of art. 25(2) DSA is a case in point and certainly fails to provide clarity about the interplay between the DSA and the UCPD.

section_path: Call for Evidence: Digital Fairness Act

evidence_quotes.quote: Dark patterns that harm consumers are prevalent across the digital sphere.

paragraphs.text: Dark patterns that harm consumers are prevalent across the digital sphere. The regulation of dark patterns under the Digital Fairness Act (DFA) should therefore extend to all traders. This would close existing gaps in consumer protection.

section_path: Call for Evidence: Digital Fairness Act
Scope of the Digital Fairness Act

evidence_quotes.quote: dark patterns can affect all groups of consumers.

paragraphs.text: Empirical research indicates that dark patterns can affect all groups of consumers. For example, Zac et al. (2025) test the susceptibility of different user groups to a range of dark patterns and find that (i) all user groups can be adversely affected by dark patterns and (ii) dark patterns are effective in influencing user decision-making regardless of users' income, education or age. $^{1}$ Kugler et al. (2025) find that "dark patterns significantly and adversely affect all kinds of Americans—the rich and the poor, the old and the young, men and women, and both highly-educated and less-educated people." $^{2}$ They also find that "consumers cannot fully overcome dark patterns even when they try." These findings strengthen the case for legislative intervention.

paragraphs.text: Empirical research has shown that dark patterns occur across e-commerce websites, social media, chatbots, mobile apps and games. The definition of dark patterns in the DFA should capture this breadth and apply to the "structure, design, function or manner of operation of

evidence_quotes.quote: interfaces that are generated, adapted or personalised through algorithmic and AI-based systems.

paragraphs.text: a digital interface or a part thereof". This formulation is technology neutral. It is compatible with and complementary to Recital 67 DSA ("the structure, design or functionalities of an online interface or a part thereof") and art. 4(4) Data Act ("the structure, design, function or manner of operation of a user digital interface or part thereof"). This formulation also captures interfaces that are generated, adapted or personalised through algorithmic and AI-based systems.

section_path: Call for Evidence: Digital Fairness Act
Scope of the Digital Fairness Act

evidence_quotes.quote: dark patterns

paragraphs.text: My analysis of EU consumer law provisions that expressly regulate dark patterns—art. 25 DSA, art. 16e Consumer Rights Directive (CRD), arts. 4(4) and 6(2)(a) Data Act, art. 22 CRD, art. 15 Consumer Credit Directive II (CCD II) and the Key Information Document in the PRIIPs Regulation—shows that they are informed by empirical findings about consumer heuristics and biases and their exploitation by business practices. These provisions protect consumers whose behaviour deviates from the benchmark of a rational actor. I have called this the "biased consumer image". For example, art. 25(3)(a) DSA acknowledges that framing effects, which are ignored by a rational agent, can influence consumer decision-making. Whereas the "average consumer" is an important market actor who plays a central role in removing barriers to cross-border trade between Member States and completing the EU's internal market, the biased consumer requires legal protection from having their biases and heuristics exploited. Compared to protecting the average consumer, protecting biased consumers emphasizes the protection of consumers as weaker parties. To ensure continuity with existing provisions that expressly regulate dark patterns, the DFA should likewise protect consumers who are susceptible to the exploitation of their heuristics and biases by business practices. As empirical research indicates, this includes all groups of consumers.

section_path: Call for Evidence: Digital Fairness Act
Scope of the Digital Fairness Act

evidence_quotes.quote: dark patterns

paragraphs.text: Similar to the general legislative approach of the UCPD, the DFA should contain (i) principles-based general prohibitions designed to address dark patterns and (ii) a clear blacklist of prohibited dark patterns. This general approach combines the benefits of principles-based and rules-based regulation. The latter provides ex-ante certainty and the former ex-post closing of gaps in protection. A principles-based general prohibition requires a case-by-case assessment. It needs to be flexible enough to address new and emerging problematic conduct. The proposed blacklist of prohibited dark patterns addresses issues of legal uncertainty and gaps in protection compared to the status quo as described by the Fitness Check.

section_path: Call for Evidence: Digital Fairness Act

evidence_quotes.quote: Creating legislation that regulates dark patterns is an example of empirically informed lawmaking, which requires robust empirical evidence.

paragraphs.text: Creating legislation that regulates dark patterns is an example of empirically informed lawmaking, which requires robust empirical evidence. The prohibition of a specific dark pattern should be supported by robust empirical data demonstrating the harmful effects of that practice. This is not merely an empirical issue; it also brings the definition and scope of consumer harm to the forefront (see para. 12 below). As more empirical data becomes available, the blacklist should be reviewed on a regular basis. The requirement of robust empirical evidence does not preclude the adoption of a principles-based general prohibition, since such data can inform the case-by-case assessment of the harmful effects of particular commercial practices.

section_path: Call for Evidence: Digital Fairness Act

evidence_quotes.quote: Art. 25(1) DSA and art. 16e CRD already contain a principles-based general prohibition expressly designed to capture dark patterns.
In other words, the existing provisions in EU consumer and digital law that expressly regulate dark patterns address specific instances of autonomy violations.
Hence, the regulation of dark patterns in the DFA should likewise recognise and capture loss of autonomy as a harm.

paragraphs.text: Art. 25(1) DSA and art. 16e CRD already contain a principles-based general prohibition expressly designed to capture dark patterns. This general prohibition differs from the general prohibition of art. 5(2) UCPD, which focuses on economic harms. My analysis of art. 25(1) DSA, art. 16e CRD and other provisions in EU consumer law that expressly regulate dark patterns has shown that these provisions adopt autonomy as a normative lens through which to assess dark patterns. In other words, the existing provisions in EU consumer and digital law that expressly regulate dark patterns address specific instances of autonomy violations. Hence, the regulation of dark patterns in the DFA should likewise recognise and capture loss of autonomy as a harm. This would ensure complementarity and consistency with existing EU law.

section_path: Call for Evidence: Digital Fairness Act

paragraphs.text: My recommendation is that the principles-based general prohibition of dark patterns in the DFA should be aligned with the normative principles used in art. 25(1) DSA. This achieves complementarity and consistency with existing legislation. This could be expressed as follows:

evidence_quotes.quote: deceives or manipulates consumers

paragraphs.text: the structure, design, function or manner of operation of a digital interface or a part thereof shall be prohibited if it deceives or manipulates consumers or otherwise materially distorts or impairs the ability of consumers to make free and informed decisions.

section_path: Call for Evidence: Digital Fairness Act

evidence_quotes.quote: existing EU law provisions that expressly regulate dark patterns

paragraphs.text: The Call for Evidence notes that the DFA will work “in full complementarity” with existing legislation protecting consumers online. My analysis of existing EU law provisions that expressly regulate dark patterns has identified six categories of autonomy violations in B2C relationships (listed in the next paragraph). These categories specify the types of harm to autonomy caused by dark patterns. I have developed these categories against the background of the information paradigm and the conception of autonomy that underpin EU consumer law.

evidence_quotes.quote: dark patterns violate consumer autonomy

paragraphs.text: As I explain further in my article, dark patterns violate consumer autonomy if they:

evidence_quotes.quote: Influence consumers’ decision-making in such a way that consumers ignore or misunderstand mandated information;

paragraphs.text: a. Influence consumers’ decision-making in such a way that consumers ignore or misunderstand mandated information;

evidence_quotes.quote: Cause consumers to hold false beliefs that form the foundation of consumers’ decision-making;

paragraphs.text: b. Cause consumers to hold false beliefs that form the foundation of consumers’ decision-making;

evidence_quotes.quote: Cause consumers to enter into a specific contractual agreement with a business without reflection about its substance and content;

paragraphs.text: c. Cause consumers to enter into a specific contractual agreement with a business without reflection about its substance and content;

evidence_quotes.quote: d. Create unreasonable time, decision effort, or emotional costs for pursuing or adhering to a particular decision;

paragraphs.text: d. Create unreasonable time, decision effort, or emotional costs for pursuing or adhering to a particular decision;

evidence_quotes.quote: Present choice options in a non-neutral manner when asking consumers to select between different choice options or

paragraphs.text: e. Present choice options in a non-neutral manner when asking consumers to select between different choice options or

evidence_quotes.quote: Manipulate consumers.

paragraphs.text: f. Manipulate consumers.

evidence_quotes.quote: The six categories of autonomy violations provide a normative classification of dark patterns for the purposes of EU consumer and digital law.

paragraphs.text: The six categories of autonomy violations provide a normative classification of dark patterns for the purposes of EU consumer and digital law. In terms of their granularity, they are placed between detailed rules that target specific cases of dark patterns and a principles-based general prohibition.

evidence_quotes.quote: addressing dark patterns in EU consumer and digital law.

paragraphs.text: These categories of autonomy violations fulfil multiple purposes. First, they can be used to interpret the general prohibitions in art. 25(1) DSA, art. 16e CRD and the DFA. This achieves consistency between the different legislative acts addressing dark patterns in EU consumer and digital law. Second, if the Commission intends to create a middle layer of prohibitions between a general prohibition and a blacklist—akin in granularity to arts. 6 to 9 UCPD—these categories can serve as a starting point for determining what such prohibitions should capture. Third, blacklisted dark pattern practices in the DFA that fall within these categories of autonomy violations would be consistent with existing EU consumer and digital law, including the dark pattern provisions already contained in EU consumer and digital law.

evidence_quotes.quote: drip pricing
difficult cancellations of subscriptions
auto-renewals
free trials converted into paid subscriptions

paragraphs.text: The DFA should contain specific, clearly formulated dark pattern prohibitions as a blacklist. The Call for Evidence mentions the following specific dark patterns: (i) drip pricing, (ii) difficult cancellations of subscriptions, (iii) auto-renewals and (iv) free trials converted into paid subscriptions. This Part assesses whether these practices should be blacklisted.

evidence_quotes.quote: Drip pricing refers to the practice of gradually revealing additional, optional or mandatory fees during the purchase or checkout process as opposed to disclosing such fees up front, making the total price of a product or service higher than the low headline price which was revealed to the consumer at the beginning of the purchasing process.
This reasoning supports the proposition that the practice of dripping mandatory fees creates unreasonable costs for consumers and violates consumer autonomy.

paragraphs.text: Drip pricing is prevalent in live events, hotel and airline industries. Drip pricing refers to the practice of gradually revealing additional, optional or mandatory fees during the purchase or checkout process as opposed to disclosing such fees up front, making the total price of a product or service higher than the low headline price which was revealed to the consumer at the beginning of the purchasing process. Drip pricing violates consumer autonomy when it (i) influences consumers' decision-making in such a way that consumers ignore or misunderstand mandated information, (ii) causes consumers to hold false beliefs that form the foundation of consumers' decision-making or (iii) creates unreasonable time, decision effort or emotional costs for pursuing or adhering to a particular decision. Point (i) occurs when drip pricing affects consumer decision-making in such a way that consumers do not correctly understand the total price of the product or service or ignore the total price (and use the headline price instead) when making purchasing decisions. Point (ii) occurs when the design of the decision environment induces the false belief that the initial (headline) price represents the total price of the product or service. This assessment remains unchanged even if the false belief is corrected at a later point in the purchasing process. In relation to point (iii), research shows that consumers may complete the purchasing process even if they had not started this process had they known about the total price at the beginning of the purchasing process. This shows that the time, decision effort or emotional costs that a consumer invests in the purchasing process can make it difficult for consumers to pursue the decision to discontinue the purchase once the total price is revealed. Whether the additional costs are unreasonable is an evaluative decision. Consumers cannot avoid mandatory fees, and it is possible for a business to reveal those fees at the beginning of the purchasing process. It is difficult to see a reason for revealing mandatory fees during or at the end of the purchasing process, apart from taking advantage of behavioural factors. This reasoning supports the proposition that the practice of dripping mandatory fees creates unreasonable costs for consumers and violates consumer autonomy.

evidence_quotes.quote: dripping of mandatory fees during the purchasing process

paragraphs.text: These are strong reasons to consider the practice of dripping of mandatory fees during the purchasing process a blacklisted practice.

evidence_quotes.quote: “Subscription traps” refer to the design of a digital interface in such a way that it effectively deters consumers from unsubscribing to a service by creating unreasonable time, decision effort or emotional costs.
the design of a digital interface in such a way that it effectively deters consumers from unsubscribing to a service by creating unreasonable time, decision effort or emotional costs.

paragraphs.text: “Subscription traps” refer to the design of a digital interface in such a way that it effectively deters consumers from unsubscribing to a service by creating unreasonable time, decision effort or emotional costs. For example, consumers had to go through multiple cumbersome steps to cancel their Amazon Prime subscription before Amazon changed its cancellation practice in the EU in 2022 after a dialogue with the European Commission. Such steps involved scrolling through multiple pages that contained unclear button labels, warnings that deterred users from cancelling, skewed wording, confusing choices and complicated navigation menus.

evidence_quotes.quote: “not allowing users to cancel subscriptions through the same medium that was used to initiate the subscription.”

paragraphs.text: The Commission should consider the following a blacklisted practice: “not allowing users to cancel subscriptions through the same medium that was used to initiate the subscription.”

evidence_quotes.quote: Subscription traps could also be targeted by prescribing a mandatory cancellation function or button for traders offering subscriptions.

paragraphs.text: Subscription traps could also be targeted by prescribing a mandatory cancellation function or button for traders offering subscriptions. This could be drafted against the background of the withdrawal function in art. 11a CRD.

evidence_quotes.quote: cancelling a subscription should be as easy as signing up for one.
unreasonable decision-making costs (in terms of time, effort or emotional costs) when cancelling a subscription
structure, design, function or manner of operation of the digital interface effectively deters consumers from pursuing their decision to cancel the subscription.

paragraphs.text: A risk in prohibiting subscription traps is legislative “overshooting”. For example, it is sometimes said that cancelling a subscription should be as easy as signing up for one. This viewpoint appears to be backed up by the wording of art. 25(3)(c) DSA (“making the procedure for terminating a service more difficult than subscribing to it”). However, the mere finding of an asymmetry in the level of difficulty between subscribing to and cancelling a subscription does not in itself establish a violation of autonomy. Consumers’ ability to make and execute an autonomous decision to cancel a subscription is not compromised simply because the cancellation process requires two simple clicks on a website, whereas signing up for the service requires only one. Autonomy theory does not demand that it must be as easy to cancel a subscription as it was to sign up to it.[10] What matters is whether consumers face unreasonable decision-making costs (in terms of time, effort or emotional costs) when cancelling a subscription, which is the case when the structure, design, function or manner of operation of the digital interface effectively deters consumers from pursuing their decision to cancel the subscription.[11] Subscription traps should only be prohibited if they warrant the legislative assessment and generalisation that they effectively deter consumers from cancelling their subscriptions. A specific cancellation process that does not effectively deter consumers

evidence_quotes.quote: Dark Patterns

paragraphs.text: [10] Martin Brenncke, *Regulating Dark Patterns*, 14 NOTRE DAME JOURNAL OF INTERNATIONAL & COMPARATIVE LAW 39, 72 (2024).

evidence_quotes.quote: dark pattern warranting legal prohibition.

paragraphs.text: from unsubscribing may still be considered an inconvenience, but it does not violate consumer autonomy and should not be regarded as a dark pattern warranting legal prohibition.

evidence_quotes.quote: Design features such as pre-checked boxes in digital environments that default consumers into the automatic renewal of a product or service, thereby incurring recurring costs, violate consumer autonomy.
automatic renewal of a product or service, thereby incurring recurring costs,

paragraphs.text: Design features such as pre-checked boxes in digital environments that default consumers into the automatic renewal of a product or service, thereby incurring recurring costs, violate consumer autonomy. The Commission should consider such dark patterns as a **blacklisted practice**. Such a prohibition would align with art. 22 CRD and art. 15 CCD II. Both provisions regulate default options such as pre-ticked boxes because they are assumed to cause consumers to enter into a specific contractual agreement with a business without reflection about its substance and content. The consumer would incur financial costs of which they are unaware, and these costs add normative weight in favour of regulating such autonomy violations.

evidence_quotes.quote: default consumers into converting a free trial into a paid subscription at the end of the trial period.

paragraphs.text: The Commission should consider the following as a **blacklisted practice**: design features such as pre-checked boxes in digital environments that default consumers into converting a free trial into a paid subscription at the end of the trial period. The rationale for this recommendation is the same as set out in the previous paragraph.
